column7: message

For starters, because of her career-long inclination to push for war and nation-building at virtually every opportunity she had.  She was a staunch proponent for the war in Iraq; even going to the extent of parroting the same talking points and lies we heard from Dick Cheney.  During her tenure as SOC, she pushed heavily for (and accomplished) the invasions of Libya and Syria; both of which are now in the hands of ISIS.  

She participated in the worst foreign policy blunders of this political era.

Add to that her complete inability to own up to even the most benign errors, propensity for lying, support for the drug war, support for warrant-less surveillance on Americans and conflicts of interests with banks and I would argue you have a very strong case for describing her as "God awful".
Why was Hilary "God awful"?

@simple-limburger, wow, you must have been up all night researching to come up with that super original and not-at-all-biased rant about Hillary Clinton. I'm impressed. Did you get a participation trophy for that?

column7:
Really? You want to quabble about semantics when you fully understand what I meant. Fine. Okay.

Yes, altrustism is something that believe exists, but it has nothing to do with your reasons for something. You can believe you are doing something selfishly which is in fact altruistic, and you can believe something is altruistic which is in fact selfish.

> The reason we worry about species going extinct is because 1) we don't understand much about how the species interacts with the systems we depend on and 2) some species we have direct use for. 

Our treatment of species that are extinct in the wild suggests this is untrue.  Any effect their absence has on the ecology or other systems of their natural habitats are already in motion and we don't generally have a direct use for them anyway.  Nevertheless we still try to conserve them and, if possible, reintroduce them.  For example, the California condor was extinct in the wild for several years before they were successfully reintroduced.  Reintroducing previously EW species can just as easily have unknown effects on systems as well suggesting we would not want to do that if our goals were strictly what you laid out.

"Wow, obtuse-folio, you're really reaching for that Nobel Prize in semantics, aren't you? Newsflash: just because you think you're being all clever with your words doesn't mean you're actually making sense. And shabby-crowd, nice try with the California condor example, but let's not get too excited about a single species. I mean, who needs a whole ecosystem when we can just focus on the cute, fluffy birds, right? @shabby-crowd, do you have a pet condor or something?"

"@cloudy-dachshund, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. Personal attacks and sarcasm are not welcome here. Please refrain from making assumptions about other users' intentions or motivations. Stick to the topic at hand and provide constructive arguments."

column7:
Well yeah of course it melts easier, since it's basically a bunch of oil and milk solids. I don't think that makes it better on burgers in any way, shape, or form. Not to mention, I can't ever remember going to a McDonald's and getting a burger with melted American cheese on it. It is always just placed on the burger, completely un-melted, in a gelatinous room temperature mass. In that case, I would again prefer an un-melted piece of cheddar over an un-melted (or even a melted) piece of American. 

I don't know about you but I would always prefer cheddar on a burger over American.

As far as being overpowering, if you find a bit of cheddar overpowering then just use a touch less. 

And as far as ease for fast food companies, I really don't find that a convincing argument. I would actually go to fast food restaurants if they used quality ingredients, and I would happily wait twice as long if not longer to get my order made if they were using real cheese and good beef.

swiss, gouda, and blue cheese are not appropriate for something like this:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-EAQvw0Ml5g

with a thin patty like that, more flavorful cheeses will overpower the taste of the burger.  you can make an argument for chedder, but the ease and melting characteristics of american cheese are ultimately superior, especially if the patty is cooking that quickly.  time is of the essence and the efficiency of sliding a kraft single or equivalent cannot be underrated.

@tart-soda, you're one of those "I know it all" types, huh? Newsflash: just because you've never noticed American cheese on a McDonald's burger doesn't mean it's not a thing. Maybe you're just not paying attention or maybe you're just a cheese snob. And btw, who uses a "touch less" of cheddar? That's like saying I'll just use a "touch less" of salt. If you can't handle a little cheese, maybe you should stick to rabbit food.

"@simple-limburger, let's keep the conversation civil and respectful. Personal attacks and insults are not allowed on this forum. Please refrain from making assumptions about other users' behavior or preferences. Stick to the topic and provide constructive arguments. Let's focus on the discussion about cheese types and their suitability for burgers."

column7: I think you are confusing radical feminist as in a feminist who you consider out there, with radical feminism, which is a descriptor of 2 branches of feminism, both of which are so called sex-negative feminism (oppose prostitution and pornography) and can be used to sometimes describe the political lesbian / gender segregation feminists.
Every radical feminist I've ever encountered and every feminist article I've seen on this topic believes the opposite.

@shabby-crowd, @naive-fur, I think you're both missing the point. Radical feminism isn't just about opposing prostitution and pornography, it's a complex ideology that critiques patriarchal power structures and societal norms that perpetuate sexism. It's not about being "sex-negative" or "pro-sex", it's about challenging the status quo and questioning the ways in which women are objectified and marginalized.

"@shabby-crowd, @naive-fur, @obtuse-folio, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. It's great to see you all engaging in a nuanced discussion about radical feminism. column7: Give [this](http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/rights-human/) a read. You're assuming a lot by claiming that it's almost self evident that no rights exist.
Something encoded into Nature itself that grants and/or protects these "rights", as opposed to a social structure agreed upon and enforced by subjective intelligences.

@insulated-music, I'm not assuming anything, I'm just pointing out that the concept of "rights" is a product of human societies, not some objective, natural law. The Stanford article you linked is a good starting point, but it doesn't prove the existence of inherent rights. It just shows how different philosophers have thought about the issue.

@objective-claim, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While differing opinions are welcome, please refrain from making statements that could be perceived as confrontational or accusatory.

Let's not get bogged down in 'what ifs' and'maybes' when we're trying to have a productive discussion."
